name: El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop
name_original: Las Médulas desde Puente de Domingo Flórez – circular por Ponferrada y Puerto de El Morredero
description: This difficult 90.1-mile (145.1 km) road cycling loop takes you through the challenging El Morredero Pass and the historic Las Médulas.
region_intro: Road cycling around Puente De Domingo Flórez features a landscape characterized by significant elevation changes, river valleys, and mountainous terrain. The region offers challenging ascents and descents, with routes often traversing through areas with substantial climbs. Cyclists can expect to encounter varied topography, including passes and riverine environments. Best road cycling routes around Puente De Domingo Flórez The most popular road cycling route is Puerto Fonte da Cova from Puente de Domingo Flórez – circular by La Baña, a 50.0 miles (80.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 4 minutes to complete. This difficult circular route features substantial elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop, a difficult 90.1 miles (145.1 km) path. This long-distance loop explores high-altitude areas and historical landscapes, requiring significant endurance. Local road cyclists also love the Cosmo from La Barosa - circular by the Burbia and Sil rivers, a 40.4 miles (65.0 km) trail leading through river valleys, often completed in about 3 hours 31 minutes. Road cycling around Puente De Domingo Flórez is defined by mountain passes, river valleys, and historical sites. The network offers options primarily for advanced ability levels, with all listed routes being difficult. The routes in Puente De Domingo Flórez are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 130 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Puente De Domingo Flórez's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes around Puente De Domingo Flórez?
A: Road cycling around Puente De Domingo Flórez is characterized by significant elevation changes and mountainous terrain. All routes listed are classified as &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt;, featuring challenging ascents and descents that require good physical condition and experience.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Puente De Domingo Flórez area?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Puente De Domingo Flórez, all of which are designed for experienced cyclists due to their challenging nature.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Puente De Domingo Flórez?
A: Yes, several routes are circular. Notable options include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046234679/puerto-fonte-da-cova-from-puente-de-domingo-florez-circular-by-la-bana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Fonte da Cova from Puente de Domingo Flórez – circular by La Baña&lt;/a&gt;, which is 50.0 miles (80.4 km), and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046247431/cosmo-from-la-barosa-circular-by-the-burbia-and-sil-rivers" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cosmo from La Barosa - circular by the Burbia and Sil rivers&lt;/a&gt;, covering 40.4 miles (65.0 km).

Q: What kind of distances can I expect on road cycling routes here?
A: The routes in Puente De Domingo Flórez vary in length, generally ranging from approximately 40 miles (65 km) to over 90 miles (145 km). For instance, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046239875/el-morredero-and-las-medulas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 90.1 miles (145.1 km) ride.

Q: What are the best times of year for road biking in Puente De Domingo Flórez?
A: Given the mountainous terrain, the best times for road cycling are typically spring and autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the potential for snow or colder conditions in winter, especially at higher altitudes.

Q: What interesting sights can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region offers a mix of historical and natural attractions. You might encounter sites like the ancient Roman gold mines of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4612988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, or explore natural features such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5550699" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo&lt;/a&gt;. The routes often traverse river valleys and offer scenic viewpoints.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by lakes or reservoirs?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate beautiful water features. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31851750" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo – Sobradelo Medieval Bridge loop from A Barosa&lt;/a&gt; specifically includes &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5550699" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Puente De Domingo Flórez?
A: The road cycling routes in Puente De Domingo Flórez are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 130 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area's varied and challenging terrain.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options in this area?
A: Due to the significant elevation changes and the classification of all listed routes as &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt;, the road cycling options around Puente De Domingo Flórez are generally not considered suitable for families or beginners. They are best suited for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge.

Q: What kind of elevation gain should I expect on these routes?
A: Road cycling in Puente De Domingo Flórez involves substantial elevation gain. Routes can feature climbs exceeding 2,000 meters, with some, like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046239875/el-morredero-and-las-medulas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop&lt;/a&gt;, reaching over 3,600 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there any historical sites accessible by road bike?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046239875/el-morredero-and-las-medulas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, explores historical landscapes including the famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4612988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Médulas Roman Gold Mines&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: The Peñarrubia Reservoir contains the waters of the Sil River. It is located on the border between León and Galicia, next to the N-536 National Highway, which runs between Ourense and Ponferrada.
Lago de Carucedo is a small town near the border of Galicia and León with a lake of the same name at the foot of the Sierra de Enciña de Lastra
Orellán viewpoint from where you have the best panoramic views of Las Médulas, in the Bierzo region.
Cycling along the N-536 national road, you can glimpse the defensive wall of the medieval castle of Cornatel and its towers on top of the hills.

This bridge, which connects the neighborhoods of Toral and Merayo, is built over the Oza River and combines traditional architecture with the natural beauty of the area. Its slate and limestone masonry structure evokes a sense of historical authenticity. Although it is not as famous as other medieval bridges, its uniqueness and location make it an interesting place to visit. 😊
García Ojeda bridge crossing the Sil river near the Castle of the Templars in Ponferrada, León. Spectacular views of the river and the town.
The castle consists of a large polygonal enclosure (more than 8,000 square meters) with double and triple defenses forming barbicans, towers, rooms and a large patio or albacar.

The 13.87 km climb to Puerto del Morredero (Alto de Cruz) from Valdefrancos is very hard due to its length, gaining 854 m at an average 5.9% and reaching a height of 1,451 m above sea level. Go at your pace.
A 6km climb from the Cabo river to Corporales gaining 430m with an average gradient of 7.4% is a tough climb reaching 1250m above sea level
Puente de Domingo Flórez is a small town on the border between Galicia and León where the Cabreira River flows into the mighty Sil River.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop?
faqs.answer: This road cycling loop is rated as difficult and requires very good fitness. It features substantial length (over 145 km) and significant elevation gain (over 3,600 meters), including challenging climbs like the Puerto del Morredero, which is an HC (Hors Catégorie) rated climb from Ponferrada.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows well-paved roads, but be prepared for some segments that might be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to push your bike. The climbs are consistent and can reach high altitudes, demanding mental strength in addition to physical fitness.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a variety of stunning landscapes. Key highlights include the beautiful climb up the Peñalba slope, the unique red clayey mountains of Las Médulas (a UNESCO World Heritage Site), and panoramic views from the Mirador de Orellán. You'll also pass by the Peñarrubia Reservoir and Lago de Carucedo.

faqs.question: What historical and natural landmarks will I see on this loop?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in history and natural beauty. You'll cycle through the ancient Roman gold mining landscape of Las Médulas, with its distinctive red earth formations and chestnut groves. Other notable landmarks include the challenging El Morredero Pass, Ponferrada Castle, and the Medieval Bridge of Toral de Merayo.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop?
faqs.answer: The tour starts near Puente de Domingo Flórez. Parking availability can vary, so it's advisable to check local parking options in the village or nearby towns like Ponferrada, which is a significant point on the loop.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the significant climbs and potential for high altitudes, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with milder temperatures. Summer can be very hot, and winter may bring snow or ice to the higher elevations of El Morredero Pass.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling tour, which may not be ideal for dogs. If you plan to visit specific natural areas like Las Médulas, it's best to check local regulations regarding pets, as rules can vary. Generally, dogs should be kept on a leash in protected areas.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route or the natural park?
faqs.answer: You do not typically need a permit to cycle the general road network of the El Morredero and Las Médulas Loop. However, Las Médulas is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and while access to the general landscape is free, specific attractions like the Orellán Galleries may charge an entrance fee. The route passes through the region of El Bierzo, which is adjacent to the Parque Natural da Serra da Enciña da Lastra, but the core cycling loop itself doesn't require specific park permits or fees.

faqs.question: Does this road cycling route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * San Cristóbal de Valdueza, [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * Nogar, and [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * Peñalba de Santiago. You'll also find smaller segments of the Camiño de Inverno and [CIMA LE17] Mirador de Orellán.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the loop passes through several towns and villages, including Ponferrada and Puente de Domingo Flórez, you will find options for cafes, pubs, and accommodation. It's advisable to plan your stops, especially on the longer, more remote sections of the climb.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: For this demanding route, ensure you have sufficient water and nutrition, as resupply points can be sparse on climbs. Essential gear includes appropriate cycling attire, a repair kit, navigation tools, and layers for changing weather conditions, especially at higher altitudes. Given the difficulty, a good level of mental preparedness is also key.
